lines.text: Chapter one, section five. Scenario: Going to the Movies.
Now we move into the first of the book's interactive scenarios — a situation drawn from everyday social life that turns out to have a surprisingly rich game-theoretic structure.
The setup: two friends, Andrew and Blanca, have decided to go to a movie together.
Andrew would prefer to see a drama; Blanca would prefer to see a comedy.
But here's the key: for both of them, the most important thing is being together.
Andrew would rather see a comedy with Blanca than watch a drama by himself.
Blanca would rather see a drama with Andrew than watch a comedy by herself.
Company trumps genre for both of them.
This has all the ingredients of a game in the game-theory sense: multiple players, each choosing from multiple options, and payoffs for each player that depend on what both of them decide.
Andrew can choose comedy or drama.
Blanca can choose comedy or drama.
What each person gets out of the evening depends on both of their choices, not just their own.
One important detail the book asks you to accept: assume both players make their choices independently, without being able to consult each other first.
You can imagine Andrew lost his phone after they made plans, so they can't coordinate.
Andrew might know Blanca's strategy — he might know she tends to flip a coin, for example — but he won't know the actual result of that coin flip.
The choices happen in isolation.
Now, to do any math, we need to put numbers on the preferences.
For Andrew: his best outcome is seeing a drama with Blanca, which is worth five points.
His second-best outcome is seeing a comedy with Blanca — still together, just not his preferred genre — worth four points.
Going to a drama alone is worth one point.
The worst outcome for Andrew is going to a comedy alone — he misses out on both his preferred genre and his preferred company — worth zero points.
For Blanca, the preference ordering is mirrored.
Her best outcome is seeing a comedy with Andrew, worth five points.
Going to a drama with Andrew is second-best, worth four points.
Going to a comedy alone is worth one point.
Going to a drama alone is the worst for her — worth zero points.
We can organize all four possible outcomes into a two-by-two grid.
The rows represent Andrew's choices and the columns represent Blanca's choices.
In each cell, the first number is Andrew's payoff and the second is Blanca's.
If both go to the comedy: Andrew gets four, Blanca gets five — they're together at Blanca's preferred film.
If Andrew goes to comedy but Blanca goes to drama: Andrew gets zero, Blanca gets zero — they're both alone at a film they chose.
If Andrew goes to drama but Blanca goes to comedy: Andrew gets one, Blanca gets one — again, both alone.
If both go to the drama: Andrew gets five, Blanca gets four — they're together at Andrew's preferred film.
The grid makes the whole structure of the game visible at a glance.
Now for the questions this scenario asks you to work through.
First question: suppose both players always go to the comedy — Andrew goes to comedy, Blanca goes to comedy, every time, as a pure strategy.
Is this an equilibrium?
How can you tell?
Think about it using the definition: can either player unilaterally deviate and get a better payoff?
Second question: what if Andrew's strategy is always to go to the drama and Blanca's strategy is always to go to the comedy?
Is that an equilibrium?
Again, apply the definition — can either player do better on their own?
Try to work through both questions before moving to the answers section.
The reasoning process is at least as important as the conclusions, so think carefully about the structure of the argument, not just the final answer.
Keep in mind that the equilibrium test requires checking whether a unilateral change helps — not whether some combination of changes by both players might produce a better overall outcome.
That distinction is going to matter for at least one of these questions.
